data.title: FB Financial: Stock Is Down, But A Dividend Machine
data.description:
We continue the pivotal Q1 regional bank earnings today with FB Financial Corporation (NYSE:FBK). The stock has retraced with the sector, and this pullback means new buyers are going to get a better yield. As for our past coverage, we can tell you that previously opened a trade in this stock in 2023. After the stock surged, we sold the initial investment and took 20% of the profit, and let the rest run as a house position. We love executing trades and then keeping profit in the run for the long term, collecting all future, growth, dividends, etc.

We currently rate the bank a hold at these levels, but see the coming merger with Southern States Bancshares as a net positive. Let us discuss the FBK Q1 report and outlook.

FB Financial headline Q1 performance

FB Financial reported a top line, which grew 21.6% from a year ago to $130.6 million, thanks to strong key metrics. Many of the key metrics we follow continue to improve. Loans held for investment were up from the sequential Q4, while deposits also increased. The margins continue to be strong. Net interest margin was 3.55%, 5 basis points higher than the sequential Q4. Margins have been around this 3.5% level for several quarters. FB Financial reported adjusted net income of $40.1 million, or $0.85 per share, which was flat from Q4, and a once cent beat versus estimates.

FB Financial loans and deposits

Total assets were $13.1 billion, flat from the $13.1 billion in the sequential quarter, but up from $12.5 billion in the year ago quarter. Total loans held grew from $9.60 billion at the end of Q4 to $9.77 billion at the end of Q1. Net loan growth was led by increases in commercial and industrial loans ($91.8 million in growth), consumer and other loans ($67.9 million), and in multifamily loans ($54.7 million in growth). There were also increases in residential real estate loans at $21.8 million in growth. Deposits also increased very slightly. Deposits were $11.2 billion, flat from Q4. Overall, solid progress in what is usually a slow quarter.

FB Financial's return metrics

During Q4, the return on assets improved to 1.21% from 1.14% in Q4. The return on average equity rose to 10.1% from 9.6%, and on a tangible basis, the return on average equity rose to 11.9% from 11.1% in Q4. We also saw an improvement in nonperforming loans, which decreased to 0.79% of all loans from 0.87% in the sequential Q4. In addition, we saw a decrease in nonperforming assets as a percentage of total assets to 0.84% from 0.93% in Q4.

The President and CEO Christopher Holmes noted in the release some concern, too:

The Company continued to optimize the balance sheet, appropriately managing growth, liquidity and credit while maintaining core deposits and delivering loan growth for the quarter. Net interest income was impacted by fewer business days, but we managed margin and liquidity by reducing higher cost deposits. Our markets, including our two new additions of Asheville, North Carolina and Tuscaloosa, Alabama, are gaining momentum and should help us achieve our relationship growth goals in 2025......The Company continues to maintain ample capital for both organic growth and strategic opportunities. In the first quarter, we demonstrated this by executing share buybacks and announcing our pending merger with Southern States Bancshares, Inc. We are well-positioned for opportunities, as well as uncertainties, and are alert for opportunities to deploy capital.

Folks, make no mistake about it. The company still has a fortress balance sheet, and the company has the capital to deploy. They have solid return metrics, are growing loans, and have a pending merger which is going to help the company grow.

Looking ahead

FB Financial Corporation is a great dividend growth name. The yield is getting higher as the stock comes down. While the yield is low, it is getting better, and the dividend constantly gets raised. The stock is down with the sector.

As for the merger, SSBK shareholders will get 0.8 shares of FBK stock in exchange for the merger. This merger will add 15 branches to FB Financial, adding them in Alabama and Georgia. Overall, we still have a hold rating, but on further weakness, this will be a regional bank to consider buying again.

data.title: Netflix Q1 Preview: Big Tariff Winner
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

While the markets have been volatile for the first 3 and a half months of 2025, I continue to be bullish on streaming giant Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ:NFLX), especially in the midst of the raging worldwide tariff war started by President Trump. Since my last piece of coverage on Netflix in mid-January, shares are up 11.34% and are outpacing the market by roughly 19 percentage points, given that the market is down about 8.34% in that same time frame.

Netflix has continued to surge even in the middle of a very tough time for the overall U.S. economy. A blockbuster Q4 has set the company up for a great 2025.

Netflix added roughly 19 million new subscribers last quarter.

Although the company currently has nearly 300 million subscribers, management continues to insist that Netflix is “just getting started,” noting its just 10% share of TV viewing time. I believe it leaves a massive runway for Netflix to be able to improve and capture even more of the TV viewing market.

On the surface, streaming subscriptions feel like a discretionary purchase for consumers. Wouldn’t streaming service companies hurt in the event of a trade war that causes inflation and hurts consumers?

Ironically, the answer is no. Streaming services are increasingly the go-to option for Americans to get access to essentially “cheap entertainment.”

With this, Netflix’s use of AI in content optimization only took this one step further in Q1, with recommendation quality only increasing. I believe Netflix stands out as one of the best low-cost substitutes for entertainment. This should allow them to do well.

Heading into Q1 earnings later this week (April 17th), I continue to believe that shares of the streaming service are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

The recent tariff surge that has been spearheaded by President Trump, has created an unanticipated yet powerful tailwind for Netflix. These tariffs have caused inflationary pressures to build up in import supply chains. Consumers are starting to make tough choices.

According to a February article published by EMarketer, 76% of U.S. consumers say they will cut back on spending if goods become more expensive. The data was provided by CivicScience. According to the data, eating out could be one of the first things to go, should consumers start cutting back on their spending.

Dining out could be one of the biggest casualties of tariffs: Half of consumers said they would cut back on fast food, while 46% plan to reduce fast-casual or full-service spending.

Discretionary spending, particularly on experiences, is also likely to be hard-hit.

Here’s what’s notable: according to EMarketer’s research, there was no mention of streaming services being a place consumers cut.

Why is that? I think the answer lies in the value consumers receive from a Netflix subscription.

Because while most assumptions would point to consumers becoming “cheap” in an economy dominated by tariffs, I think this is the wrong way to think about it. Consumers in a slower economy are seeking goods and services that give them a lot of value.

This is the biggest reason I am doing follow-up coverage. Consumers are thinking more about "value" today than they ever have before. Netflix is extremely well-positioned to benefit.

Q1 Preview

Netflix is expected to announce its Q1 earnings on Thursday after the bell. Current EPS estimates are very encouraging. According to a consensus of 34 analysts, the company is expected to post an EPS of $5.74. If this bottom-line number is met, this would represent an 8.80% increase in earnings. Frankly, I think this is too conservative. While most of the tariff news hit in Q2, Netflix has an incredible content slate they’re working on. This should push subscriptions (and EPS) much higher than this.

Meanwhile, 35 analysts project that Netflix will haul in $10.51 billion in revenue this quarter. This would mark 12.14% year-over-year growth for Netflix.

Advertising has become a huge part of the story for Netflix. Over 55% of new signups in markets with ads have opted for the ad-supported plan, which I discussed at length in my last piece of research. Ad revenue doubled year-over-year in 2024. Netflix plans an even broader rollout of its proprietary ad-tech stack in the U.S. starting this month.

According to a recent industry conference hosted by Morgan Stanley, the streaming service’s management sees 2025 as the shift from “crawl to walk” in ads.

On this same note, subscriber and content trends remain strong. Subscriber engagement likely remained high in Q1, driven by a solid blend of original hits and popular returning franchises. According to an article published by AlphaStreet, Netflix “improved product/market fit across all regions.” To me, this suggests stronger subscriber retention globally.

AI personalization features like content suggestions and thumbnails keep churn low and viewer satisfaction high. On this same note, Netflix is testing new OpenAI search capabilities that let users search for new content based on their mood or hyper specific terms.

Better matches between content and user preferences means much better user retention. All of this in time for a tariff war that’s likely set to leave consumers dining at home more.

Big Tariff Winner

Tariffs on imports are driving up prices on physical goods. According to CBS News, some products like footwear are seeing 20% tariffs. Trumps broad across the board 10% tariffs are starting to bite into consumer leisure activities.

According to one article published by Quartz, Netflix is explicitly expected to be one of seven companies to be listed as a “Trump trade war” winner. The article explains why Netflix will benefit from the tariffs:

Why does the streamer make the list? If prices climb too high elsewhere — restaurants are likely to be severely impacted by tariffs — many consumers may just decide to stay home and chill. There won’t be a tariff on your Netflix subscription.

As I talked about in my most recent piece of analysis on Netflix, the company started the year by expanding its family-friendly and live entertainment content slate. The streaming giant added licensed blockbusters like Inception, and it even started live-streaming WWE wrestling. I believe that as Netflix continues to enter into the live sports field — like it plans on doing by streaming the FIFA Women’s World Cup — this will enhance the appeal for cord-cutters. This will make Netflix an even more appealing “stay at home” option. For many companies, tariffs are a headwind. Netflix is about to benefit from a strong tailwind.

Valuation

Netflix currently has a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 36.97. When compared to the sector median of 11.62, this is a 218.18% premium. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Netflix a grade of a D- on this metric. Although this may seem expensive at first glance, I think it is more than justified.

The company’s forward estimated revenue growth rate of 13.70% is a strong 390.44% premium to the sector median, which is currently just at 2.79%. On a forward Non-GAAP PEG basis, Netflix trades at 1.58, which is only a 38.58% premium over the sector median of 1.11. To me, this suggests that the streaming giant’s growth is happening at a reasonable price for investors.

The stock’s price-to-earnings premium, which is about 3x the sector median, is still below the revenue growth premium, which is roughly 5x the sector median. To me, this suggests that a re-rating is necessary. If the valuation adjusts to reflect growth more fully, I believe the company’s forward PEG ratio should trade at a 60% premium to the sector median. With this, if we saw Netflix’s forward PEG ratio rate at a 60% premium (up from the current 38.58% premium), this would represent 55.52% upside for shares.

Risks

I feel good that Netflix is in a great position to succeed, I’ll admit it’s not risk-free. Netflix’s success will depend heavily on delivering hit pieces of content. A weak slate could quickly reverse subscriber momentum even in a tariff war. With this, I think YouTube is a huge risk. The online video company remains a top competitor to Netflix, as both companies are fighting for screen time, especially among younger audiences.

Consumers typically see YouTube’s content as free. As content on YouTube gets more and more premium (YouTuber Mr. Beast is an excellent example of this), Netflix has to distinguish itself by being hyper-focused on how it defines premium content and how they differentiate themselves.

Over the long run, I believe that Netflix will continue to distinguish themselves with premium, curated content. I think the best way they can do this is to refine their search and content serving algorithms. Already, we’re seeing Netflix incorporate OpenAI into their tech stack to better align content that serves user moods. I wrote more about this AI push last fall. The key here is that both Netflix and YouTube now both have content libraries so large that it's not possible to watch all the content on their platforms in one lifetime.

Now, it's up to their algorithms to serve you the best content that you like. This is the key to winning. Netflix is investing heavily here.

Bottom Line

While markets are volatile, I continue to hold a strong buy rating on Netflix and I think this is a great place for investors to hide out as the U.S. engages in a once-in-a-generation trade war. I struggle to find many companies with this incredible blend of strong earnings growth, true macro tailwinds from tariff-fueled shifts, and new monetization levers like advertising and live events. And with the company pushing for a $1 Trillion market cap by 2030 (up from $398.3 billion today) management is aligned with shareholders for stronger growth. This really seems to be a company firing on all cylinders.

I think that Netflix offers an intriguing GARP story: a premium valuation but outsized growth, with strong upside if re-rated. I think earnings will be a catalyst. I think the PEG multiple has room to expand from here.

As we approach Q1 earnings on Thursday, I believe shares remain a strong buy.

data.title: Jaguar Mining: This Cat Doesn't Hunt
data.description:
All figures are in United States dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day.

Q4 2024 & FY2024 Results

Jaguar Mining (OTCQX:JAGGF) (TSX:JAG:CA) released its Q4-24 results in March, reporting quarterly production of ~14,800 ounces, a 20% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to a temporary suspension of operations at its Turmalina Mine in Brazil (~4,300 ounces produced), offset by slightly higher production at Pilar (~10,500 ounces). The latter was related to higher throughput at similar grades (3.65 G/T of gold vs. 3.68 G/T of gold).

Unfortunately, the improved year-over-year production at Pilar wasn't enough to offset lower output from Turmalina, with Jaguar Mining producing just ~64,700 ounces last year. Not only was this a 9% decline year-over-year, but it was a near 13% miss vs. its soft guidance of 74,000 ounces, and the company's fourth consecutive miss on guidance. Unfortunately, the company's Q1-25 results have continued this trend of declining production with Turmalina still offline, with Q1-25 production of ~9,900 ounces, representing a 39% decline in year-over-year production on a company-wide basis.

(*) Soft guidance of 74,000 ounces based on multiplying 18,500 ounces (Q4-23) by four quarters, per Jaguar's 2024 statements. (*)

As for the company's Q4-24 and FY2024 financial results, quarterly and annual revenue came in at ~$159 million and ~$42 million, respectively. This translated to 16% and 18% increases in year-over-year revenue, though entirely driven by external factors (record gold price) and not at all related to operational execution. Meanwhile, operating cash flow improved to ~$57 million in 2024 (+58% year-over-year), while free cash flow improved to ~$16 million vs. a free cash outflow in the year-ago period.

Overall, this resulted in Jaguar Mining ending the year with a ~$46 million cash position, but working capital was roughly flat year-over-year, impacted by a ~$24 million increase in current liabilities with the net impact of the Satinoco tailings pile slump and current reclamation provisions. As of the end of Q1-25, net cash slid to ~$33 million from ~$39 million, impacted by the Satinoco tailings pile slump.

Costs & Margins

Moving over to costs and margins, Jaguar Mining has continued to see a steady trend higher in operating costs, reporting Q4-24 and FY2024 all-in sustaining costs of $1,737/oz and $1,651/oz, respectively. Both unit costs figures benefited from higher ounces sold than produced, with ~16,000 ounces of gold sold in Q4-24 vs. ~14,800 ounces produced. Hence, adjusting for actual production, Q4-24 all-in sustaining costs would have come in closer to $1,900/oz on the ~14,800 ounces produced.

Fortunately, the record gold price helped to push margins higher despite another disappointing operationally, with Q4-24 and FY2024 AISC margins coming in at $904/oz (~34.2%) and $735/oz (~30.8%), respectively. This was a meaningful improvement from $472/oz and $318/oz in the year-ago period, with Jaguar Mining benefiting from a record gold price of $2,641/oz in Q4-24. Unfortunately, the company will diverge from its peers this year and be selling significantly fewer ounces in the best time ever to be selling gold given that Turmalina remains offline, suggesting 2024 production will drop at least 20% year-over-year and come in closer to 50,000 ounces.

While some investors might cheer the margin expansion, the below chart puts this win in context. As we can see, Jaguar saw a $34/oz improvement in AISC margins in 2024 vs. 2020 ($735/oz vs. $701/oz) in a period where the gold price gained nearly $700/oz on an average realized basis. This is disappointing, to say the least, and while many producers have done an exceptional job capturing excess margin from the gold price, Jaguar Mining has done a brilliant job of doing anything but capturing excess margin, which should be the ultimate goal of any commodity producer as periods of commodity price strength don't last forever.

Unfortunately, 2024 AISC of $1,651/oz (FY2023: $1,618/oz) benefited from a weaker Brazilian Real relative to the US Dollar (UUP), but this tailwind has since left. This is not ideal from an operating cost standpoint. The only good news is that Jaguar Mining has elected not to provide production or cost guidance this year in a period where it's now working against a rising BRL/USD rate, so at least it technically can't miss for a 5th consecutive year if it doesn't provide guidance.

Recent Developments

2025 Outlook & Track Record

Following the announcement of the Satinoco tailings pile slump in December, Jaguar Mining shared on December 9th that the timing of restarting operations at its Turmalina Mine was unknown, but that it wouldn't be shorter than one month. Four months later, Jaguar has shared that though efforts to stabilize the pile are “progressing well”, the timing of the resumption of operations is unknown, placing a huge cloud over the 2025 outlook.

On a positive note, its operating Pilar Mine is expected to see closer to 40% of ounces from the higher-grade BA structure this year, up from 20% in 2024. Still, even if we assume higher production year-over-year at Pilar to make up some of the difference and a very minor contribution from test mining at Santa Isabel, I would be surprised to see Jaguar produce over 50,000 ounces this year. This declining production profile leaves much to be desired, and it's going in the wrong direction relative to long-term guidance provided in January 2022 of 135,000 ounces by early 2027, per its statement below.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"Current expectations, based on present day assumptions, in particular for gold prices and permitting timelines, see us adding on a probability weighted basis approximately 45,000 ounces of annual production [~54% growth] from the growth areas described below over the next 5 years."

- January 17th, 2022

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Assuming Turmalina restarts next year, Faina will allow for a recovery to previous production levels, but there's obviously little hope of delivering into the implied goal of ~135,000 ounces by Q1-27 based on the 2022 guidance midpoint of 90,000 ounces + "adding 45,000 ounces" per the January 2022 Growth Plan announcement.

Plus, this temporary suspension of operations at Turmalina has certainly affected the ramp-up of Faina, which began producing in Q2-24 but was expected to require 15 months to ramp up to full production. So, even if Turmalina operations are restarted in Q4-25, we're likely to wait until December 2026 earliest to see Faina at full production levels (1,000 TPD) until adequate vertical access is established.

Satinoco Dry-Stack Pile Slump

The most recent development and certainly the most negative is the Satinoco dry-stack tailings pile slump that occurred in early December and has taken operations offline at Jaguar's second mine. Not only does this increase the stock's risk profile, as it is now effectively a single-asset producer relying entirely on the small-scale Pilar Mine, but it could have significant ramifications from a financial standpoint, which we'll discuss below.

Starting with the event, though, Jaguar Mining has stated that it needs to stabilize the Satinoco pile to reduce the risk of further movements and continues to work with regulators to satisfy safety and community support requirements. As for progress on site, Jaguar has constructed a 400-meter-long containment barrier around the slump material, with four sumps constructed to water runoff around the slumped material.

The company is also working on determining the causes of the failure (shown above), with potential causes being:

  • Failure of the foundation of the pile
  • Planar failure of the pile
  • Overload on the pile
  • Possible influence of water
  • Possible collapse of a mine crown pillar

Jaguar expects that the report will not be available until Q4-25, and it's quite possible that it will not go the go-ahead to restart until it is determined what caused this significant slump that forced the evacuation of mine personnel and the neighboring community (288 residents and 118 families had to be relocated), and which affected five houses in the Casquilho community, with a “modest” impact on non-critical mine infrastructure. Impact to mine infrastructure included a maintenance shed, piping feeding the filter plant, outdoor storage areas and an emergency exit from the mine being covered.

While not nearly as disastrous as the heap-leach failure at Copler that has kept this mine in Turkiye sidelined, this is still a significant incident for a mine of this much smaller scale and I would be surprised to see operations back to full production levels before the end of Q3-25.

Outstanding Fines

As for outstanding fines, they have been considerable, and these can be added to existing fines already in place. Looking at existing fines first, Jaguar shared it received an unfavorable judicial decision from the Minas Gerais State Court, ordering it to pay ~$3 million for assessed environmental damages related to the rupture of a tailings pipeline at Paciencia in 2011. The company also received a final decision from Brazil's Superior Labor Court (TST) to fine Jaguar for breaches of labor law related to employees working on holidays between February 2015 and October 2019. This relates to lacking pre-authorization from Brazil's Ministry of Labor, with fines in the amount of ~$5 million.

Unfortunately, fines related to the Satinoco tailings pile slump are far greater, with the State Environmental Secretariat and the office of the Public Prosecutor handing down infractions amounting to a total of R$520 million or ~$88 million at a BRL/USD rate of 0.17/1.0. This is over double Jaguar's current net cash position and, while it does not expect to incur significant cash outlays in 2025 related to these fines, it certainly leaves a dark cloud over the stock.

Overall, these fines look to be on the excessive side, and it's quite possible that Jaguar could settle for a smaller amount. On this note, Jaguar Mining has signed an agreement with the Public Defender's Office of Minas Gerais to fairly determine compensation for individuals/families affected by the incident. Assuming all impacted individuals and families submit claims in line with the Fair Compensation Parameters, Jaguar estimates compensation costs of ~R$57 million or ~$10 million.

While this would be a positive outcome vs. what the market might be expecting, the bigger fine to watch will be the environmental fine of R$320 million, which at ~$54 million would be a huge hit to Jaguar Mining if it can't be renegotiated lower. Plus, this is in addition to other existing fines totaling ~$8 million relating to breaches of labor law and environmental damage (2011 incident).

To summarize, Jaguar is hardly out of the woods here and while a simple look at free cash flow (2025 estimates: ~$21 million) and NPV might suggest the stock is undervalued, the stock is much less attractively valued when allocating for these outstanding fines, even if they come in at half the amount and can be renegotiated lower.

Valuation

Based on ~82 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$2.15, Jaguar Mining trades at a market cap of ~$176 million and an enterprise value of ~$143 million. This continues to leave it as one of the smaller market cap producers in the sector today and one of the cheaper producers, trading at less than 6x FY2025 EV/FCF estimates. Normally, this would present a buying opportunity, but with Jaguar Mining and, as I have been warning since US$7.00/share, this is a stock that is cheap for a reason and an inferior way to get leverage to the gold price.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 3.5x FY2025 cash flow estimates and 0.80x P/NAV (8%) for a small-scale Tier-2 jurisdiction producer with all of its NAV tied to a single country and one of the worst track records of sector-wide in terms of delivering on promises, I see a fair value for the stock of US$2.45.

This assumes a minimum $20 million penalty (current fine from SES = ~$51 million) and a higher payment to resolve this issue could materially reduce this fair value estimate. It would also be extremely detrimental for the company when combined with ~$9 million in expected community payments, with the SES fine and community payments depleting most of its current net cash balance.

(*) Fair value estimate is based on a 65/35 weighting to P/NAV vs. P/CF. (*)

If we measure from the current share price of US$2.45, this points to a 14% upside from current levels, suggesting Jaguar Mining could make a run towards its 200-day moving average if it were to trade back to its fair value estimate. However, for micro-cap producers and especially those with inferior track records, I prefer a minimum 50% discount to fair value to ensure a margin of safety. After applying this required discount, Jaguar sits nowhere near a low-risk buy zone today and would need to decline below US$1.24/share to become interesting from a reward/risk standpoint.

Obviously, there are no guarantees that the stock retests or breaks its recent lows. That said, if I'm going to buy the worst-run names in the sector, I prefer to get the right price or pass entirely and with one of the worst track records of delivering on guidance sector-wide, a steeply declining production profile and significant uncertainty related to outstanding fines (labor, environmental damages, recent tailings pile slump), I see far more attractive bets elsewhere.

Summary

Jaguar Mining had another disastrous year in terms of delivery on guidance, missing soft guidance by ~13% and roughly 10%, even adjusting for the Satinoco tailings slump in early December and temporary suspension of operations at Turmalina. In an unpredictable industry like mining, an occasional miss can be forgiven, but Jaguar has now missed guidance for four consecutive years and sports the worst track record of beats/misses sector-wide, missing by an average of 14.2% per annum in the period.

These are pitiful results, and in a sector where there's over 50 producers to choose from, I don't see any reason to stoop to owning the worst simply because it's trading at a lower multiple than peers. In fact, the discounted multiple largely reflects the market's correct view that short-term and long-term guidance can't be trusted, and it doesn't help that Jaguar has no NAV diversification in terms of its risk score, with all of its cash flow coming from a single jurisdiction.

Unfortunately, 2025 will see another year of declining production and while Jaguar will generate free cash flow thanks to a record gold price. However, simply generating free cash flow is quite a low bar at $3,000/oz gold prices. Further, clouds will continue to hang over the stock, including:

1) No clear date for a restart at Turmalina

2) No confirmation of the ability to reduce the significant fines handed down related to the recent incident

Additionally, it is no help from a balance sheet strengthening standpoint that we'll see multi-year lows for production at a time when the rest of the sector is thriving and maximizing production to sell into record gold prices.

Given this less favorable outlook, high-risk score and low-quality score overall, I continue to see Jaguar Mining as cheap for a reason and I don't see enough of a margin of safety to justify paying above US$2.20 for the stock. Overall, I continue to favor other producers sector-wide that have 80% to 150% upside to fair value with far better track records, and I continue to see Jaguar Mining as an Avoid.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MGE Energy: This Small Utility Looks Substantially Overvalued
data.description:
MGE Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:MGEE) is a regulated electric and natural gas utility that serves customers in south-central and western Wisconsin:

As we can see, MGE Energy does not have an especially large service area in terms of geographic size. However, it is actually fairly populous due, at least in part, to the company’s service territory encompassing one of the largest cities in the state of Wisconsin – Madison. Madison, Wisconsin, alone has an estimated population of 283,700 people, which makes it one of the largest cities in the upper Midwest. The company states that its entire service territory has a population of 369,000, so Madison alone accounts for around three-fourths of the population that the company serves. Naturally, though, not all the people in the service area have their accounts, since many people do not live alone. The company claims that it has approximately 167,000 customers, which would make it one of the smallest utility companies that we have discussed in this column in recent weeks.

With that said, it is worth noting that MGE Energy does have more businesses under its umbrella than just the regulated utility (the Madison Gas & Electric Company):

However, nearly all of these are related in some way to the utility company. For example, these other companies own things such as a cogeneration facility, electric transmission grids, or real estate that the company purchased in the event that it needs to expand its utility plants. The regulated utility operation is the main business, although it only provided just over half of MGE Energy’s earnings per share in the fourth quarter of 2024:

As we can see, for the three-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the regulated electric and gas utility only earned about $0.36 per share, about 59% of MGE Energy’s total. Thus, the regulated utility business is the most important, but the company’s other businesses do provide an important contribution to the company’s profitability. As such, we should not ignore any of its businesses, but the fact that most of them support the utility does suggest that we can evaluate MGE Energy as we would evaluate many other utilities.

Comparison With Peers

As just stated, MGE Energy only has a customer count of approximately 167,000, so it is one of the smaller utilities that we have discussed. Despite this, the company’s market capitalization of $3.32 billion is actually in line with that of some larger utilities:

Company Name

Market Capitalization

Enterprise Value

MGE Energy

$3.32 billion

$4.10 billion

Southwest Gas Holdings (SWX)

$5.18 billion

$10.19 billion

ONE Gas (OGS)

$4.56 billion

$7.84 billion

Otter Tail Corporation (OTTR)

$3.35 billion

$4.10 billion

TXNM Energy (TXNM)

$4.90 billion

$10.78 billion

NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE)

$3.51 billion

$6.60 billion

The comparison with Southwest Gas Holdings is particularly stark, as that company has a customer count of 2.226 million, or more than ten times what MGE Energy possesses. However, its market capitalization is only 35.91% higher than that of MGE Energy. The company’s market capitalization is also identical to that of NorthWestern Energy Group, which serves approximately 470,300 electric and 305,000 natural gas customers, once again a multiple of MGE Energy’s total. NorthWestern Energy Group does have a higher enterprise value, though, which suggests that it uses substantially more leverage in its business structure. Thus, its valuation as a company is still higher than that of MGE Energy, despite the similar market capitalization.

Still, this comparison does clearly show that MGE Energy is much more highly valued when compared to its actual customer count than that of its peers, which could suggest that investors may want to be cautious here. We should take a closer look at the company to make a more accurate judgment call about this, though.

MGE Energy has done a very good job of rewarding its shareholders over the past decade, especially when compared to its peers above. As we can see here, the company’s stock has delivered a 157.63% total return over the past decade:

The only one of these peer companies to manage a better performance was Otter Tail, and that was mostly because of the incredible performance that its manufacturing division delivered during the first few years following the pandemic that were characterized by abnormally high demand for that business unit’s plastic products. As I explained in a previous article, it is unlikely that Otter Tail will continue to perform as well going forward. Thus, historically, MGE Energy has delivered a relative performance that most investors would likely find very attractive, and this may at least partially explain the company’s apparent high valuation relative to some of its peers with much higher customer counts.

About MGE Energy

As mentioned earlier, MGE Energy is primarily a regulated electric and natural gas utility that serves the city of Madison, Wisconsin, and a few other areas in the southern and western parts of the state. The bulk of the company’s operations are in Wisconsin, as the company’s website states that nearly all the companies under its umbrella own assets located solely within Wisconsin. This differs from some other utilities (and all the companies shown in the peer group above), as many larger utilities operate in more than one state.

Wisconsin has fairly positive demographics, although it is certainly not the fastest-growing state in the country. According to the World Population Review, the state currently has an estimated population of 5,991,540 and is growing at a 0.51% annual rate. This is expected to continue, bringing its population up to approximately 6.1 million by 2029:

While this is generally better than some states such as Vermont or West Virginia that actually have declining populations, a 0.51% annual rate is not nearly enough to put Wisconsin among the most rapidly growing states in the United States. Indeed, the state is actually ranked in the bottom-half of the country for population growth.

Madison, which, as we have already seen, is by far the most important city for MGE Energy’s utility business, is only slightly better than Wisconsin as a whole in terms of population growth. The World Population Review states that it currently has a population of approximately 283,700 people and is growing at a 0.6% annual rate. That is only sufficient to bring the population up to 290,500 by the end of the decade:

Once again, this could be worse, but it does not provide the same level of demographic tailwinds as a company such as Avista Corporation or TXNM Energy possesses. Despite this, MGE Energy has had some success at growing its customer base over time. This is especially notable with the company’s natural gas utility, which has seen its customer base grow at a 1.8% compound annual growth rate since 2020:

We can see that the company’s natural gas utility has grown at a much more rapid rate than its electric utility. This is actually not that surprising since it is easier for a natural gas utility to generate growth. After all, nearly everybody in the United States has electric service to their homes and businesses. However, not everybody has utility natural gas service. A lot of people use other methods of heating homes, including propane, heating oil, and even electricity. In the state of Wisconsin, only 65.47% of homes are heated with natural gas. 11.71% of the state’s homes are heated with propane, and a higher 16.73% are heated with electricity. According to several sources, including the U.S. Department of Energy, it is cheaper to heat a home with natural gas than with either propane or electricity. As such, people may consider switching to natural gas when it becomes available in their area. Thus, a natural gas utility can actually generate customer growth by expanding its network and converting people who previously used another heat source over to natural gas. It is much harder to generate customer growth significantly in excess of population growth by adding new customers to the grid due to the simple fact that there are very few people in the United States today who do not already have electric service.

With that said, one thing that can be a driver of growth for an electric utility is new businesses opening up in its service territory. Presumably, a new business has not had electric service before (even if the owner of the business has such service at their home). Thus, the new business represents a new customer for the utility and this allows them to grow their customer base at a faster rate than population growth or the rate of new household formation. There are not really any good statistics on the rate of new business openings in Madison, but the Madison Downtown Business Improvement District provides a list of eight businesses that opened in just the months of January and the first half of February 2025. The same organization states that eleven additional businesses are scheduled to open in the very near future. Madison, Wisconsin is also considered an innovation center for the biotechnology industry, so that will typically result in new businesses starting up. Thus, it is certainly possible that MGE Energy’s electric utility operation is benefiting from an entrepreneurial culture featuring numerous start-up businesses that are allowing it to grow its customer base at a more rapid pace than the population in its service territory is growing. However, without hard data, this is mere speculation.

However, the overwhelming majority of the company’s electric customers are residential users and not businesses:

These figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, so they are reasonably current. As we can see, approximately 87% of the electric utility’s account holders are actually residential and not business customers. However, the majority of the company’s revenues actually come from businesses. The fact that most of the firm’s revenues come from businesses is not particularly surprising. After all, most businesses use considerably more electricity than an individual’s or a family’s residence. Meanwhile, there are usually more residential properties than business properties in a given area. After all, many businesses have more than a single employee, and everybody needs a place to live.

MGE Energy does not provide any breakdown of customer growth by segment, though, so we cannot make a determination of how much of an impact new business creation had on the company’s customer growth. The figures above are almost identical to what the company reported for the full-year 2023 period, however:

The only difference between the 2023 and the 2024 numbers is that residential electric volumes were very slightly lower in 2024 than in 2023 as a percentage of the total electricity consumption in the company’s service territory. This suggests that businesses, or whatever is included in the “Other” category (government, perhaps), increased their consumption at a slightly faster rate than did residential customers. This adds a very small amount of support to the thesis that non-residential customers have been contributing to the company’s growth and allowing it to grow its customer base at a faster rate than the population growth is increasing.

Rate Base Growth

As we saw above, MGE Energy reports that its natural gas customer base is growing at a 1.8% compound annual growth rate, while its electric customer base is growing at a 1.3% compound annual growth rate. This is unlikely to get any investor excited about the company, since we would all like any business that we are invested in to deliver a much higher growth rate than that. After all, we can all grow our nominal wealth at a much greater than 1.8% annual rate just by parking it in a savings account at the local bank. Many companies in other industries are able to grow their earnings at a rate between 10% and 20% fairly consistently, so it would be incredibly off-putting if the only growth that a utility could deliver was at a rate equivalent to its customer growth.

Fortunately, MGE Energy can deliver a much higher growth rate by investing money into upgrading, modernizing, and expanding its utility infrastructure due to the fact that regulators allow a utility to increase its prices so that it can generate a specified rate of return based on the value of its infrastructure. MGE Energy recently unveiled a plan to invest approximately $1.400 billion into its infrastructure over the 2025 to 2029 period:

In yesterday’s article on Unitil Corporation (UTL), I was somewhat concerned about that company spending $980 million on capital investments over the same period, and here we have a company that is planning to spend 42.86% more. However, I am not as concerned about it in this case because of the fact that MGE Energy is a much bigger company. In the case of Unitil, the $980 million planned spending actually exceeded the company’s entire market capitalization.

That is not the case here, as $1.400 billion is only 42.17% of MGE Energy’s market capitalization. As such, MGE Energy should actually have an easier time affording its planned capital investments than will Unitil Corporation. This is obviously a good thing for risk-averse investors.

One thing that we notice above is that the majority of the company’s planned capital spending is dedicated to constructing new generation capacity to support the company’s carbon-reduction goals and not to new distribution lines (which would improve the company’s ability to support customer growth). Overall, MGE Energy is directing $845 million, or 60.36% of its total spending, towards the construction of new electric generation facilities. This includes nine new solar plants that are scheduled to come online between now and the end of 2028:

As well as three wind farms scheduled to come online in 2027:

The company is also constructing six battery storage facilities to try and cover for the fact that wind and solar are unreliable:

It is rather difficult to make a case for solar power making sense to deploy at all in Wisconsin, given that the sky is either cloudy, rainy, or overcast for a substantial proportion of the year. This chart shows the average percentage of the days for which cloud cover is under 60% in three areas of Wisconsin:

As we can see, there are only five months out of the year in which the sky is clear, mostly clear, or partly cloudy (less than 60% of the sky is covered by clouds) for more than 50% of the days. The majority of time, in most months, there is cloud cover over 60% of the sky or more. This reduces the effectiveness of solar power and makes it somewhat hard to justify actually deploying the generation capacity.

With that said, the state’s regulators will allow MGE Energy to raise the rates that it charges for electricity to cover the costs of constructing these inefficient solar farms, so investors might not care too much about the economics of deploying solar panels in an unsuitable climate. Indeed, the company already secured regulatory approval for a 4.17% increase in the base rate for 2025, which comes on the heels of a 1.54% increase in 2024. It will likely continue to be the case that the company will get approval for rate increases as it spends money and finishes these solar farms that are currently under construction.

With that said, MGE Energy is one of the few utilities that has not provided a long-term growth outlook. Over the past five years, it has grown its earnings per share at a 5.8% compound annual growth rate:

If we assume that it is trying to maintain the same slow and steady growth rate going forward (about a 6.6% annual growth rate in its rate base and a 5.8% annual growth rate in its diluted earnings per share), then investors can expect a 7% to 8% total average annual return, which is lower than many of its peers have positioned themselves to deliver over the same period. The biggest reason why this projected total return is lower than the 8% to 12% that is typical for utilities is that MGE Energy has a very low dividend yield of 1.98%, though, not because a 5.8% earnings per share growth rate is excessively low. This could suggest that this company is overvalued right now.

Financial Considerations

MGE Energy has a very strong balance sheet with a relatively low level of debt. We can see this by comparing the company’s net debt-to-equity ratio to that of its peers:

Company Name

Net Debt-to-Equity Ratio

MGE Energy

0.63

Southwest Gas Holdings

1.31

ONE Gas

1.06

Otter Tail Corporation

0.45

TXNM Energy

2.24

NorthWestern Energy Group

1.08

There are very few utilities that have a net debt-to-equity ratio of less than 1.0, and we can clearly see that MGE Energy is one of them. This company is less reliant on debt to finance itself than nearly all of its peers, and this should be taken as a net positive. Ultimately, it could prove advantageous to the company in terms of financing its capital expenditures, since it may be able to limit the equity dilution that other utilities are forced to impose on their shareholders because it can lever up a bit more.

Perhaps due to the company’s very low level of debt and limited use of it in financing its capital spending projects, MGE Energy has done a very good job of limiting the impact that the rising interest rates over the past few years have had on its business. Here are the company’s total and net interest expenses over the past eleven quarters:

(Figures in millions of U.S. dollars.)

Over the period, the company’s net interest expenses only increased by 26.15%, which is far lower than the increase that its peers experienced over the same period:

Company Name

% Change in Net Interest Expense Q2 2022 to Q4 2024

MGE Energy

+26.15%

Southwest Gas Holdings

+36.00%

ONE Gas

+138.04%

Otter Tail Corporation

+17.78%

TXNM Energy

+104.23%

NorthWestern Energy Group

+47.50%

Once again, we can see that MGE Energy outperforms all of its peers in this respect, with Otter Tail again being the sole exception. The company’s generally low debt level and low reliance on debt to finance its operations prove to be an advantage to it, as it limits the extent to which debt service payments drag down its earnings.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, MGE Energy has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 23.94 at the current share price. This is quite high for a utility company in general and higher than the rest of the peer group:

Company Name

Forward P/E Ratio

MGE Energy

23.94

Southwest Gas Holdings

19.26

ONE Gas

17.88

Otter Tail Corporation

18.56

TXNM Energy

18.76

NorthWestern Energy Group

15.96

(All figures from Zacks Investment Research.)

As of the time of writing, the utility sector (IDU) has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 17.41:

MGE Energy is thus clearly more expensive than any of its peers, as well as more expensive than the sector average when measured using forward earnings.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Tool also suggests that the stock is substantially overvalued:

We can see here that the stock has a Quant rating of F, which is worse than the peer group:

Company Name

Seeking Alpha Quant Rating

MGE Energy

F

Southwest Gas Holdings

C+

ONE Gas

C

Otter Tail Corporation

B

TXNM Energy

D+

NorthWestern Energy Group

B+

As shown, all of the peer companies have much more attractive valuations than MGE Energy when using this valuation metric as well.

Overall, if the company’s very low dividend yield was not a strong enough indicator that the valuation here is stretched, the above metrics appear to confirm it.

Conclusion

In conclusion, MGE Energy is a relatively small electric and natural gas utility that serves the city of Madison, Wisconsin, as well as a few other areas in the state. While that region of the country has some positive demographics, it is still experiencing weaker population growth than many other areas of the country. MGE Energy is devoting a lot of money to constructing solar farms that really do not make much economic sense in Wisconsin, but they will still likely allow the company to boost the rates that it charges customers and generate some positive earnings growth.

The real problems here are the yield and the valuation. MGE Energy’s 1.98% current yield is substantially below the 2.30% yield of the domestic utility sector as a whole. Its valuation appears to be well above the sector average. Overall, it is very hard to make a case for this company at its current valuation.

data.title: Pepsi: Pay The Right Price
data.description:
PepsiCo, Inc. (NASDAQ:PEP) is one of the most beloved stocks in the world, yet the company has underperformed the market for a long-time now. The consumer staples company even faces additional risks from HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. pushing for less consumption of unhealthy snacks. My investment thesis is Bearish on PEP stock due to stretched valuation assigned to a stock with limited growth.

Headwinds

Pepsi just reported another quarter where revenues didn't even grow, with Q4 revenues declining 0.3% YoY while missing analyst targets by $110 million. The company has long been over-loved due to the history of consistent growth and dividend hikes, but this has led to years of stock underperformance, with the market constantly overpaying for the business.

The company faces an additional risk with RFK Jr. supporting a ban on using food stamps to purchase soda. The new government official, along with others, has a big push to reduce SNAP spending on food and beverage items potentially contributing to obesity.

Pepsi already faces a tough environment hitting sales growth targets of 4% and 7% in annual EPS growth. Noteworthy, the sales targets are largely hit due to making regular acquisitions, such as the recent nearly $2 billion deal for Poppi and $1.2 billion price for Siete Family Foods back in October.

Poppi sales hit $100 million in 2023 and soared to $500 million last year. The prebiotic soda category was estimated at $440 million in annual revenues back in 2023 and includes popular Olipop, which was targeted by Pepsi back in 2024.

Coca-Cola (KO) recently launched Simply Pop to compete in the prebiotic market. Pepsi is buying into the market just as the competition is heating up and the owners of the business that built the terrific growth story are cashing out.

Pepsi is estimated to reach $92 billion in annual sales this year, so Poppi at even $900 million in sales in 2025 would only contribute 1 percentage points to the sales growth rate. The Poppi growth will account for a large portion of the actual reported growth rates, likely continuing the path for organic growth of only a few percentage points.

Either way, these deals appear to signal Pepsi remains growth challenged in the traditional beverage and snack business. The company is continuing down a path to acquire businesses in order to generate minimal growth with Bank of America cutting the stock to Neutral due to the challenged growth environment.

Overpaying For Dividend Growth

Pepsi is a solid company generating billions in annual profits. The company even targets returning $8.6 billion to shareholders in 2025, but the return levels of the stock are derived from whether an investor purchases the stock at the right price.

Over the last decade, Pepsi has vastly underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), with the benchmark index nearly doubling the total return of the beverage company. Even worse, the total return for Pepsi over the last 5 years has been a minimal 27.1%, while the S&P 500 is up 109.3%.

Even now, an investor should see the general problems facing the stock. Pepsi currently trades at 17.5x the 2025 EPS target of $8.27 while an analyst like Bank of America just predicted limited growth for the consumer stables company.

The consensus analysts generally have Pepsi producing 5% to 6% EPS growth in the years ahead. An investor is paying upwards of 3x the forecasted growth rate of the business, assuming the company actually gets to 6% EPS growth when RFK Jr. intends to reduce the purchases of beverages and snack foods.

Apparently, the low returns of the last 5 years haven't generally persuaded investors from paying a rich multiple for limited growth. Pepsi has hiked the dividend for 53 consecutive years, which now usually signals investors are likely willing to overpay for the stock.

Ironically, the dividend yield reaching the highest level in history at 3.7% isn't even the buy signal most would assume. Pepsi has even constantly hiked the dividend payout, including a recent 5% hike to $5.69 per year.

What the information highlights is how Pepsi has constantly traded at a premium valuation leading to the weak total returns. The company only produced $12.5 billion in operating cash flows last year and spent $5.3 billion on capex.

The net impact is Pepsi only producing $8.2 billion in free cash flow while planning to return $8.6 billion to shareholders this year. The company has a cash balance of $9.3 billion with an incredible $44.3 billion in debt, up from the end of 2023.

In essence, Pepsi has a stock with a 3.7% dividend yield with no remaining cash flow to reduce the debt levels. The company is spending nearly $3.2 billion in acquiring Siete and Poppi via cash this year, which Pepsi won't pay out of cash flows due to already paying the large dividend.

The numbers would support Pepsi producing solid and consistent returns, if the stock traded at a reason P/E multiple relative to the growth rate. The consumer staples company produces minimal growth, but the stock trades more at a market multiple due to investors grasping onto the Dividend Aristocrat concept of over favoring a business constantly hiking the annual dividend whether the hikes are even warranted or the stock price makes any sense.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Pepsi has now underperformed the key benchmark index for years, and the trend should continue. The beverage company still has to acquire businesses for growth while the stock trades at a premium valuation.

Investors buying Pepsi here plan to underperform the S&P 500. The stock at the right price would make sense, but Pepsi should trade closer to 10x to 12x EPS targets with a dividend yield topping 5% to be attractively priced, considering the company is paying out a large portion of operating cash flows via dividends.
